text.title: Hook techniques, concrete language, and the so-what test
text.summary: Tactical elements that make stories land: opening hooks that create questions, concrete numbers over abstractions, and the so-what test that forces every data point to carry an implication.

rule.statement: For every data point, state the implication explicitly. A statistic without a "so what" is incomplete. The implication must be specific to the audience's context, not generic.
rule.rationale: Audiences cannot reliably infer implications from raw numbers. Stating the implication removes ambiguity and prevents misreading.

example.code: 99.9% uptime = fewer than 9 hours of downtime per year. For our users: they will never see an error page during business hours.

rule.statement: Replace abstract outcome language with specific measurements. Never use "improved efficiency", "better experience", or "enhanced performance" without a number attached.
rule.rationale: Abstract language is unverifiable and unmemorable. Specific numbers are checkable and anchor the claim.

list.item: "Improved efficiency" → "Cut processing time from 4 hours to 15 minutes"

rule.statement: The first 30 seconds must create a question in the audience's mind. Use a surprising statistic, a question, a bold claim, a micro-story, or a contradiction — never start by introducing yourself or the agenda.
rule.rationale: Audiences decide within the first 30 seconds whether to engage. An agenda introduction signals "you can tune out for now."

list.item: Surprising stat: "90% of startups fail in year 3. Here's what the 10% do differently."
Question: "What if you could deploy without fear?"
Bold claim: "Meetings are broken. I can prove it in 2 minutes."
Micro-story: "Last Tuesday at 2am, our server crashed and no one knew for 4 hours."
Contradiction: "The best code is no code."

rule.statement: Analogies must map to a domain the specific audience already understands. Test the analogy domain before using it; an analogy from an unfamiliar domain adds confusion instead of clarity.
rule.rationale: An analogy works only if the audience can instantly map the source domain to the target. A mismatched analogy creates a second explanation problem.

example.note: Microservices analogy for a business audience familiar with restaurants
example.code: Think of microservices like a restaurant kitchen. Instead of one chef doing everything, you have specialists: prep cook, grill cook, plating cook. If orders spike at the grill station, you add one more grill cook — not another full chef who has to learn the whole kitchen.

antipattern.description: Burying the lead — writing extensive context before the recommendation.
antipattern.reason: Decision-makers read the first paragraph and skim the rest. A buried recommendation is often missed entirely.

antipattern.description: One data point per page rule violated — cramming multiple metrics on one slide.
antipattern.reason: Audiences can process one key point per visual; multiple points on one slide split attention and none land.

text.reference: Prompt: apply Pyramid Principle to executive summary
